segments.s563-g49: بر here functions as a preposition meaning 'in accordance with' or 'on the basis of' (literally 'on'). آیین is a noun 'custom/practice' and is governed by بر. فرخ is an adjective 'auspicious/fortunate' that qualifies the following noun phrase (here effectively qualifying the ancestral customs or the ancestors). نیاکان خویش is a genitive construction 'his ancestors' where خویش is represented by the possessive clitic ش (written separately in the segment list); the clitic attaches to نیاکان to mean 'his'.
segments.s563-g50: گزیده is a past-participle/adjectival form 'chosen/selected' (used as an attributive or collective noun 'the chosen'). سرافراز is an adjective 'lofty/ honored' modifying the chosen group. The conjunction و links that phrase to پاکان خویش ('and his pure ones'): پاکان is a plural noun 'the pure' and خویش + the clitic ش marks possession ('his pure ones'). The clause omits an explicit verb (a common poetic ellipsis); the possessive sense (his) applies to the whole coordinated group by ellipsis.
segments.s563-g17: خروش آمد از راه اروندرود is a compact clause with the noun خروش (the sound/roar) as subject and آمد (past tense 'came') as the verb; از راه + proper name (اروندرود) marks the source/direction 'from/along the way of the Arvand'. Word order is Subject–Verb–Adjunct, typical of Persian poetry where motion is foregrounded by آمد.
segments.s563-g18: به موبد چنین گفت هست این درود strings together a dative phrase (به موبد 'to the mobed') with a quotative/statement: چنین گفت (literally 'thus said') followed by هست این درود ('this is a greeting'). The verb گفت is reported speech; هست is the copula asserting existence/identity of این درود. In poetic word order the indirect object precedes the reporting verb and the identification clause.
segments.s563-g19: چنین گفت موبد بران شاه خرد behaves as 'Thus said the mobed to that wise king.' چنین 'thus' modifies گفت; موبد is the speaker (subject) and بران شاه خرد ('to that wise king') is the indirect object phrase, where بران ('upon/thereon/that') points back to someone previously mentioned. The verb گفت is simple past (reported speech) as is usual in narrative.
segments.s563-g20: که‌ای پاک‌دل نیک‌پی شاه گرد is an address/vocative. که‌ای functions like a classical vocative ('که + ای' = 'O'); پاک‌دل and نیک‌پی are epithetic adjectives 'pure‑hearted' and 'noble‑born/virtuous'; شاه گرد is a compact epithet for the king (گرد here part of an epic epithet). The sequence is adjectival modifiers followed by the title, typical of poetic direct address.
segments.s563-g21: کنون مرد بازاری و چاره جوی places a temporal adverb (کنون 'now') before the noun phrase. مرد بازاری is a compound meaning 'market‑man/merchant' (or a man of the marketplace) and چاره جوی is a compound participial construction 'seeker of a remedy/one seeking a solution' joined by و. This nominative phrase introduces the actor for the next verbal action.
segments.s563-g22: ز کلبه سوی خانه بنهاد روی contains the locative-from preposition ز (poetic variant of از 'from'), followed by the source noun (کلبه 'cottage'), then سوی خانه ('toward the house') as the directional phrase. بنهاد is past of بنهادن 'to place/set' used idiomatically with روی (face) to mean 'he set/turned his face (toward)'. The object/goal (سوی خانه) comes before the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s563-g23: چو بر دجله بر یکدگر بگذرند begins with the subordinating temporal/conditional particle چو ('when/whenas'), followed by the locative بر دجله ('on the Tigris'). بر یکدگر ('past/over one another') marks the reciprocal relation and the verb بگذرند is present/subjunctive/optative plural in narrative sense 'they pass' (classically used to narrate repeated or customary actions). The clause sets a temporal/background condition.
segments.s563-g24: چنین تنگ پل را به پی بسپرند is the main clause: چنین ('thus') modifies the action; تنگ پل را is the definite object 'the narrow bridge' (را marks definiteness/emphasis); به پی ('to the following' or 'to those in pursuit/behind') indicates the recipients; بسپرند (present/subjunctive/plural) from سپردن 'to hand over/entrust' describes the act of yielding the bridge. The syntax is object before indirect phrase then verb, typical in elevated/poetic Persian.
segments.s563-g9: This noun phrase has an adjective 'پراگنده' placed before the coordinated nouns 'گنج و سپاه' (a common poetic order). There is no verb here; the beneficiary/goal particle 'ورا' follows the whole phrase and marks 'for him' (i.e., the treasure and army were provided/arranged for him).
segments.s563-g10: 'بیاراست' is a past (perfective) third-person form meaning 'adorned/arranged' and here governs the following coordinated direct objects 'ایوان و گاه'. The enclitic 'ورا' again attaches to the noun phrase to mark the beneficiary 'for him'; the verb preceding its objects is a common poetic word order.
segments.s563-g11: 'چنین تا' = 'thus until' introduces a temporal clause; 'برآمد' is a past/inchoative verb 'came/arose' and here means 'had come into being' or 'had passed'. The following compound 'برین پنج سال' (broken orthographically into 'بر' + 'ین' + 'پنج سال' in the word list) is a demonstrative/qualifier on 'پنج سال' — 'these/five years' — so the clause reads 'until these five years came'.
segments.s563-g12: 'برافراخت' is a past (perfective) transitive verb 'he raised/erected'. It takes the following nominal phrase 'آن کودک خرد یال' as its object; 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that', and 'خرد یال' functions as a compound descriptor 'small‑maned' (classical word order allows such sequences), so the whole means 'he raised that small‑maned child’s crest' or more idiomatically 'that small‑maned child raised his crest.'
segments.s563-g13: 'نشسته' is a participial/predicative form 'seated' placed before the subject (poetic inversion). 'شبی' is an adverbial 'one night/by night'. The sequence 'نشسته شبی شاه در طیسفون' therefore reads 'one night the king sat in Ctesiphon' (lit. 'seated, one night, the king in Ctesiphon').
segments.s563-g14: This is a nominal phrase with a directional/locative clause: 'خردمند موبد' = 'the wise mobed (priest)'; 'به پیش' is a compound prepositional expression meaning 'into the presence of / before', and 'اندرون' specifies the inner chamber/inner part. Together they indicate the mobed coming into the inner presence ('the wise mobed came before [the king] in the inner chamber').
segments.s563-g15: 'بدانگه که' is a temporal subordinating phrase 'at that moment when'. Inside the clause, 'خورشید' is the subject 'the sun' and 'برگشت' is a past intransitive verb 'turned/returned'; 'زرد' is a predicate adjective 'yellow/pale', so the clause reads 'when the sun turned yellow.'
segments.s563-g16: 'پدید آمد' is a light/compound verb meaning 'appeared' (پدید + آمد literally 'came into view'). It is followed by the noun phrase 'آن چادر لاژورد' ('that tent, lazhvard/azure') where 'لاژورد' qualifies 'چادر' as color/material. Word order (verb before noun phrase) is typical in poetry: 'appeared that azure tent.'
segments.s563-g1: 'به شاهی' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/into kingship' (marking the state conferred). 'برو' is part of the phrase indicating assumption/advancement to that state (here best understood as an optative/directional element: 'to kingship'); 'آفرین خواندند' is a past plural clause 'they cried/sang “āfarīn”' with خواندند as the past plural verb. Word order places the phrase indicating the new state before the reporting verb.
segments.s563-g2: همه مهتران is the subject phrase 'all the mahterān' (the leading men/elders); گوهر is the direct object 'gems/jewels' placed before the verb (typical SOV order), and افشاندند is the past plural 'they scattered/sprinkled'.
segments.s563-g3: یکی introduces an indefinite 'a certain'; موبدی is the noun 'a mobad (Zoroastrian priest)'. بود is the past copula 'there was/was'. شهرو (literally 'of the town' / 'the town's') and به نام 'by the name' form an attributive phrase giving his provenance and the formula 'named [X]'. The sequence is subject — copula — appositive name phrase.
segments.s563-g4: A string of adjectives (خردمند 'wise', شایسته 'worthy', شادکام 'fortunate') joined by و; they are epithets describing the mobad introduced in the previous segment and follow the noun in the usual Persian adjective-after-noun pattern.
segments.s563-g5: بیامد is the past third-person 'he came.' The phrase به کرسی زرین نشست is a verb phrase 'sat on the golden seat/throne' where به marks direction/location and زرین (golden) modifies کرسی (seat/throne); نشست is the past 'sat.' The two verbs (came; sat) are coordinated actions of the same subject.
segments.s563-g6: میان پیش او is a compound prepositional expression meaning 'in front of him / in his presence' (میان + پیش intensifies the locative). بندگی را is the definite object 'the servitude/devotion' (را marks the definite object), and ببست is the past 'he bound/tied' — here idiomatically 'he bound himself' (i.e., pledged or devoted himself).
segments.s563-g7: جهان را is the definite object 'the world' (را marks definiteness/emphasis). همی داشت is an imperfective past construction 'he used to hold/kept' or 'he governed/maintained' (همی adds continuous/ongoing aspect). با داد و رای is an instrumental phrase 'with justice and counsel' describing how he governed.
segments.s563-g8: سپه را is the definite object 'the army/host.' به هر نیک و بد is a prepositional phrase 'in every good and bad [matter]' (هر = every; نیک و بد = good and bad), and رهنمای is the predicate 'a guide' / 'he guided' — here functioning as the verb-like predicate meaning he acted as guide or directed the army in all circumstances.
segments.s563-g25: بترسد is a third-person present-form with the b- prefix expressing a jussive/generalizing sense: 'let him/one fear' or 'may one fear'. The adverb چنین follows the verb for emphasis ('thus'), then the indefinite subject هرکس ('whoever/anyone'). از introduces the reason ('because of') and pairs with بیم ('fear') to form 'because of the fear/ alarm', while کوس names the source ('the drum'). The whole clause = 'Let anyone thus fear because of the drum's alarm.'
segments.s563-g26: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the verb برخروشند, a third-person plural present or jussive verb meaning 'they rise/ burst forth'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and زخم کوس is a poetic collocation: زخم (literally 'wound') is used metaphorically for a 'blow/strike', with کوس 'the drum' as its possessor. Together: 'Thus they burst forth like the drum's blow.'
segments.s563-g27: چنین is an adverb 'thus' placed before the verb for rhetorical effect. گفت here is the past verb 'said' (verb-subject order is common in poetry), شاپور is the subject following the verb, and با موبدان is a prepositional phrase 'with the mobeds' indicating the addressees/companions. (Note: the provided sense-list for گفت was noun-like; in this context it is the verb 'said'.)
segments.s563-g28: که‌ای here introduces a direct address and functions like a vocative 'O' (classical/poetic usage). پرهنر ('full of learning') and نامور ('renowned') are adjectives stacked before بخردان, an old plural/vocative meaning 'wise men' or 'the prudent ones'. The cluster is therefore a formal address: 'O renowned, learned wise-men.'
segments.s563-g29: پلی دیگر is the direct object 'another bridge' placed before the verb (poetic/flexible word order). اکنون is a temporal adverb 'now'. بباید is a modal verb 'it must / ought to' and takes the verbal infinitive زدن as its complement. Here زدن is used idiomatically with پُل to mean 'to put up/build (a bridge)'; that specific sense ('to build') is a poetic/compound use not listed among the simple senses.
segments.s563-g30: شدن appears as the verbal noun/auxiliary idea 'to be made / to become (built)' and is marked by را as a definite object. یکی modifies راه ('one / a single way'), and باز آمدن is a purposive/result phrase: باز here means 'open/available' modifying آمدن ('to come'/'to enter'), so راه باز آمدن = 'a way opened for coming/incoming.' Together the clause expresses that 'one route must be opened (for coming).'
segments.s563-g31: بدان is the fused form of the prefix ب- plus دان 'know' and functions as an imperative/infinitive 'know this' or 'be aware of this'. تا introduces purpose/result here ('so that'), followed by چنان ('thus') and زیردستانِ ما 'our subordinates' (زیردستان is the plural 'those under [our] authority'). The clause frames a consequence: 'Know this so that thus our subordinates ...'.
segments.s563-g32: گر is the conditional 'if'. از here is partitive/ source 'from / among' and combines with لشکری ('host, troops') to mean 'from/among troops'. در is the locative preposition 'in' or 'among', and پرستان (literally 'worshippers' or 'adherents' in the lexicon) is used poetically for a group or following; ما is the possessive 'our'. Grammatically this segment sets up a conditional: 'if (they are) from among troops, in our ___.' (The exact social nuance of پرستان is poetic/archaic and context-dependent.)
segments.s563-g33: This clause states a negative desiderative about leaving. به + infinitive (به رفتن) marks the action 'to go/leave'; نباشند is a negative present-subjunctive/optative third-person plural meaning ‘let them not be’ / ‘they should not be’ (here: ‘should not be sent to’). زین سان is a fixed phrase 'in this manner' (زین + سان = 'this/so + like/as') and به رنج places the motion into a circumstance, 'into/with hardship' (به + noun). Overall: 'They should not go thus into hardship.'
segments.s563-g34: This clause gives the corrective obligation. درم (dirhams) is the thing to be given; داد here functions as the verbal notion 'give' (inverted word order typical of poetry), and باید 'must / it is necessary' qualifies it. فراوان is adverbial/qualifying the quantity ('plentifully / many'), ز + گنج marks the source 'from the treasure'. The word order is poetic (object preceding verb and modal).
segments.s563-g35: همه موبدان is the subject phrase 'all the mōbeds / all the priests.' شاد گشتند is the compound verbal expression 'became happy' (گشتند = became, in past tense, third plural). سخت is an adverb here intensifying the verb ('very / strongly'), placed after the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s563-g36: This is a subordinate/explanatory clause introduced by که 'that / for / because'. سبز آمد literally 'came green' (آمد used as past 'appeared / came to be'), qualifying the reason for their joy; آن نارسیده درخت is the definite noun phrase 'that unripe tree' (نارسیده = not yet ripe/immature). Word order is standard: verb amid descriptive elements, typical of epic diction.
segments.s563-g37: یکی here functions as 'one (of them) / a certain one.' پل is an archaic/poetic connective meaning something like 'then / in turn' (placed before the verb for rhetorical effect). بفرمود is an honorific past tense verb 'he ordered/said' (third-person past), and موبد دگر is the direct object 'another mōbed' (دگر = other). The order 'subject — adverb — verb — object' follows poetic preference.
segments.s563-g38: به فرمان = 'by the command/order of' where به has an instrumental sense ('by'). آن کودک تاجور is the genitival phrase 'that child-merchant' specifying whose command it was (آن = that; کودک = child; تاجور = merchant/trader). Together this phrase modifies the verb 'bفرمود' in the previous segment (i.e., the order came from that child merchant).
segments.s563-g39: ازو ('because of him' / 'from him') is the causal agent marking why the following state occurs. شادمان شد is 'became joyful' (شادمان adjective + شد 'became' as perfective auxiliary), and دل مادرش is the possessor construction 'the heart of his mother' (مادر + possessive suffix ش = 'his mother'). Word order places the cause first, then the effected emotional state.
segments.s563-g40: بیاورد is a past-tense verb 'he brought' (third-person), followed by the compound noun phrase فرهنگ جویان 'culture-/learning-seekers' (فرهنگ = culture/education, جویان = seekers). برش is an enclitic/poetic object-marker here meaning 'to him/for it' (in context: 'brought the culture-seekers to him/there'); the clause thus reports that he caused or fetched the seekers of learning. The verb precedes its arguments in this poetic order.
segments.s563-g41: به زودی is an adverbial phrase 'soon' where به combines with زودی; the second به is a preposition meaning 'to/toward' and governs فرهنګ جایی ('a place of learning/culture'). رسید is simple past (3rd sg.) 'arrived/reached'; the subject is omitted (classical Persian third-person). Word order places time and prepositional phrase before the verb.
segments.s563-g42: کز is the relative/prepositional particle 'که از' = 'from which/whence' linking back to 'جایی'; آموزگاران is the source/agents 'the teachers'; سراندر is an adverbial compound meaning 'into (the) head/within' (i.e. absorbed into his mind); کشید is past tense of کشیدن 'to draw' used metaphorically 'drew/absorbed' (here expressed with the provided sense list). The clause omits an explicit subject and describes what he learned at that place.
segments.s563-g43: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بر هفت شد is a poetic way to say 'became seven' (i.e. reached the age of seven). رسم میدان is a noun phrase 'the custom/practice of the field/arena' and نهاد (past of نهادن) means 'placed/established' — here 'established the practice'.
segments.s563-g44: هم‌آورد here denotes bringing rivals together or holding contests (literal: 'bringing together equals/opponents'); و هم is the coordinating 'and also'; رسم چوگان نهاد repeats the verb نهاد 'established' with رسم چوگان 'the custom/practice of polo' as object. Overall the clause lists additional things he set up upon reaching that age.
segments.s563-g45: بهشتم is 'my paradise' / 'my delight' (بهشت + possessive -م) and شد is 'became'; آیین تخت و کلاه is the subject phrase 'the ritual/ceremony of throne and hat (crown)'. The verb precedes the subject here (poetic inversion), so the sense is 'the rites of throne and crown became my joy.'
segments.s563-g46: تو گفتی is 'you said' (the speaker addressing someone); کمر بست is a compound verb 'girded the waist' (کمر = belt/waist, بست = past of بستن 'to bind/gird'); بهرامشاه is the proper name (Bahrāmshāh) and follows the verb in typical Persian word order. The whole segment reports speech: 'you said, Bahrāmshāh girded his belt.'
segments.s563-g47: تن خویش را is a fronted definite object 'his body/his person' (تن = body, خویش = own/his, را = object marker). از در فخر کرد is an idiom 'to boast openly' (از در = 'from the door' = before people/publicly; فخر کرد = 'made pride/boasted'). The verb کرد forms the verbal phrase with the noun فخر.
segments.s563-g48: نشستنگه خود is 'his seat/seat of his own' (نشستنگه = place to sit/seat, خود = himself/his own). به اصطخر (به + place name) marks location 'at/to Estakhr' (اصطخر here a place-name), and کرد ('did/made') completes the verb phrase: 'he made his seat at Estakhr.'

sentences.s563-s25: In accordance with the auspicious customs of his ancestors, he chose his noble and his pure ones.
sentences.s563-s9: A roar came along the Arvand; to the mobed it said, "This is a greeting."
sentences.s563-s10: Thus the mobed said to that wise king: "O pure‑hearted, noble‑born king of the realm..."
sentences.s563-s11: Now the market‑man and seeker of a remedy turned his face from the cottage toward the house.
sentences.s563-s12: When they pass one another on the Tigris, thus they hand the narrow bridge over to those following.
sentences.s563-s5: He provided for him abundant treasure and troops, and he adorned for him the halls and chambers.
sentences.s563-s6: Thus, when five years had come, that small‑maned child raised (his) crest.
sentences.s563-s7: One night the king was seated in Ctesiphon; the wise mobed came into the inner presence.
sentences.s563-s8: Then, when the sun turned yellow, that azure (lapis‑colored) tent appeared.
sentences.s563-s1: They acclaimed him king with cries of “Āfarīn,” and all the elders scattered jewels.
sentences.s563-s2: There was a certain mobad of the town named Khordmand, wise, worthy, and fortunate.
sentences.s563-s3: He came and sat on the golden throne, and in his presence bound himself in servitude.
sentences.s563-s4: He sustained the world with justice and counsel, and guided the army in every good and bad matter.
sentences.s563-s13: Let everyone thus be afraid at the drum's alarm; so they surge up like the drum's blow.
sentences.s563-s14: Thus Shapur said to the mobeds: 'O famed, learned wise-men...'
sentences.s563-s15: Now another bridge must be built; one way must be opened for coming.
sentences.s563-s16: Know this, so that thus our subordinates — if they are among (some) troops...
sentences.s563-s17: They should not be made to go off into hardship like this; one must give plenty of dirhams from the treasure.
sentences.s563-s18: All the mōbeds became greatly joyful,
sentences.s563-s19: one (of them) then commanded another mōbed,
sentences.s563-s20: his mother's heart rejoiced because of him; he brought culture-seekers to it.
sentences.s563-s21: Soon he reached a place of learning from which he drew (knowledge) from the teachers.
sentences.s563-s22: When he turned seven he established the practice of the field (the arena); he staged contests and also instituted the custom of polo.
sentences.s563-s23: The rites of throne and crown became my delight; you said, 'Bahrāmshāh girded his belt.'
sentences.s563-s24: He vaunted his person openly and made his seat at Estakhr his own.
